excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU. Thanks for subscribing to these articles. Here, I'll be writing about my activities in the Philippines starting February 4, 2024 — the stuff I can't talk about publicly, and the raw, unfiltered reality. "So what's the Philippines actually like?" "What's studying
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU.

Thanks for subscribing to these articles.

Here, I'll be writing about my activities in the Philippines starting February 4, 2024 — the stuff I can't talk about publicly, and the raw, unfiltered reality.

"So what's the Philippines actually like?"

"What's studying in Cebu like?"

"Can you really go back to studying as a working adult?"

I'm sure quite a few of you are wondering about these things.

I'll be writing about all of this based on my own real experiences!

Self-Introduction

First, let me briefly introduce myself.

Once again, my name is MaSU.

I'm from Saitama Prefecture, and I'm barely still in my 20s. [As of 2024]

After graduating from university, I got a job at a major hotel, working about a year and a half in the restaurant division, and about six months in the web marketing division.

After working there for a full two years, I quit the company

and became a photographer, which I'd been doing as a side gig.

My photography work was going pretty well,

but I felt like I couldn't keep it up for 40 years,

and a certain business owner gave me two pieces of advice:

"Japanese traditional crafts are fascinating."

"If you can see that content and become someone who comes up with ideas from it, you'll be strong."

Based on those two pieces of advice,

in February 2021, I launched "FOMUS," a masu (traditional Japanese wooden cup) brand.

Under the vision of "creating an environment where people can freely express themselves using masu," I've been planning collaborations with various creators through "masu x ___" combinations, and organizing events.

As for me personally, I gave up my solo apartment in Tokyo,

and from November 2022 to the end of January 2024, I lived as an address hopper [bouncing between hotels and guesthouses] with no fixed home.

If I start talking about myself, the story gets ridiculously long,

so if you want to know more about me, check out this article!

https://fufu-parallel-blog.com/interview_fomus/

It's an interview article from July 2023.

Why the Philippines?

Living as an address hopper,

I met tons of people and received tons of inspiration.

Through random opportunities, I got chances to work overseas

and to introduce masu to foreigners visiting Japan.

However,

because I had never seriously studied English,

I found myself in a situation where "I could communicate, but couldn't have deep conversations."

I had things I wanted to say but couldn't fully express them,

and I couldn't fully understand what the other person was saying either..

Despite having created our own brand,

I couldn't convey the appeal of masu or the fun of FOMUS —

it was incredibly frustrating.

On top of that, somehow everyone around me could speak English except me!!

So I consulted my friends who are fluent in English,

and they suggested I try studying at a language school in the Philippines. I looked into it right away and decided to go.

Right now, I'm a photographer

and the head of a Japanese traditional craft "masu" brand.

If I add the skill of "being able to speak English"

on top of that,

it would broaden my work opportunities even more,

and most importantly, I'd be able to talk with people from overseas!!

So that's the deal —

I'm barely still in my 20s,

and I want to be able to speak and understand English by 30,

so I can work hard overseas throughout my 30s!! That's the plan.

When it comes to studying abroad,

there were actually several options besides the Philippines.

I was torn between the Philippines and Malta,

but I chose the Philippines because:

- One-on-one lessons

- Relatively affordable

- Long study hours

Those were the reasons I chose the Philippines.

I'm writing this article before leaving Japan,

so at some point I'm planning to talk about why I chose this particular school.

Where will I be and what will I do for 3 months?

Starting February 4th, I'll be attending a language school in Cebu.

On weekdays, it's an English-immersive life from morning to night.

Weekends and holidays are days off from school,

so I'll go out with friends or study in the dorm.

In my case, I also have work, so I'll probably be doing some laptop work too.

Of course, I'm not a college student on a study abroad trip,

so just learning English isn't enough for me.

So, to get the masu brand "FOMUS" known by as many people as possible,

I plan to use the content I've created over the past two and a half years

to interact and connect with everyone.

I'm going to make full use of these 3 months

to grow both myself and the brand.

So I think I'll be able to share a study abroad experience that's a little different from the usual one!

By the way, I've finished about 90% of my packing,

and one entire side of my suitcase

is packed full of FOMUS masu and content! Lol

Has anyone ever brought this many masu to a language school in the Philippines before?

I'll be documenting these 3 months of learning and business day by day, so I'd be happy if you enjoy reading along!

Thanks in advance!

MaSU

excerpt_en: Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "getting ready the day before." It's almost time. So, the day before my Philippines trip has arrived. This time, I'm flying with Cebu Pacific Airlines, taking a direct flight from Narita Airport to Cebu
body_en:
Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "getting ready the day before."

It's almost time.

So, the day before my Philippines trip has arrived.

This time, I'm flying with Cebu Pacific Airlines,

taking a direct flight from Narita Airport to the airport in Cebu.

The schedule is departing at 8:55 AM and arriving at 1:30 PM local time.

Since all articles here are paid content,

I'll be sharing the costs too.

The flight ticket for 2024/02/04 was

37,370 yen.

This includes the option for 20kg checked luggage, so it's a bit more expensive than usual.

The weak yen is definitely having an impact too.

After finishing packing, I weighed my suitcase,

and it came to about 20kg.

If it were just my personal stuff for the Philippines,

I wouldn't even need half that — 10kg,

but since I'm bringing FOMUS masu again,

one entire side of the suitcase is all FOMUS-related stuff,

and it ended up right at the 20kg limit.

So this part probably isn't very helpful as a reference.

Since the flight departs at 8:55 AM from Narita,

and for international flights you need to arrive 3 hours early,

I'm currently staying at "Sora no Yu," a hot spring facility near Narita Airport.

This place also offers capsule hotel stays,

and it's relatively affordable, so this is my first time using it.

They have hot springs and saunas, plus a rock bath,

and on top of that, Sora no Yu runs a free shuttle bus to the Narita Airport terminals,

so it's very convenient for someone like me who's departing early in the morning.

https://soranoyu.com

It's a Saturday, but the rate is 5,500 yen per night.

It's not super crowded,

and there seem to be mostly Japanese guests.

They also have a cafeteria so you won't have trouble getting food,

but there was no place to make phone calls or hold meetings.

Next time I have an early morning flight from Narita,

I'll definitely use this place again!

Finally, it starts tomorrow.

The day before the Philippines came so fast.

Honestly, I don't really have a clear picture of what life will be like — that's the truth. But the unknown makes the adventure exciting.

I'll try to write articles as much as possible,

and I'm also thinking of sharing unlisted YouTube URLs

to show you the real deal on the ground.

So,

this won't be entirely a text-based blog —

I'll be using various tools

to share my activities in the Philippines.

Alright, starting tomorrow, it's the real deal!

Article written on: 2024/02/03, 8:07 PM

excerpt_en: Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "arriving in Cebu." I've arrived in Cebu! I boarded the Cebu Pacific Airlines flight at 8:55 AM and arrived at 1:30 PM Philippine time.
body_en:
Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "arriving in Cebu."

I've arrived in Cebu!

I boarded the Cebu Pacific Airlines flight

at 8:55 AM,

and arrived at 1:30 PM Philippine time.

After picking up my luggage and everything, I made it outside by 2:10 PM.

It's currently 31 degrees in the Philippines.

Since it was freezing in Japan,

I had a HeatTech layer on underneath,

so it was seriously hot.

But there's a gentle breeze, so it's not muggy — it doesn't seem like it'll be unbearably hot..

Since I'm here to attend a language school,

an agent was waiting for me at the exit,

and once everyone gathered, we headed out.

It really felt like it was finally beginning.

Heading to school

Once everyone was together, we drove to the school.

The school where I'll be studying for 3 months is

CELLA.

I chose the POWER SPEAKING course at CELLA,

a program focused specifically on speaking.

At Philippine language schools,

one class is 45 minutes with a 5-minute break.

There are 10 classes every day.

As for the classes,

they don't start for another 2 days,

so as of writing this article, I haven't taken any yet

and can't share anything about the class content.

I was also shown to my room,

and since it's a 4-person room,

it looks like this.

For the arrival day,

"there's absolutely nothing scheduled."

You arrive at school on Sunday,

Monday is orientation and level testing,

and then classes start on Tuesday.

The only thing I was told was

that dinner starts at 5 PM! Lol

And the dinner window is only 50 minutes,

so if you're not there in time, no food for you!

It kind of feels like being back in school. Lol

Since today is Sunday and there are no classes,

hardly any students are around the campus,

and I'm up on the rooftop writing this article by myself.

Anyway, I safely made it to Cebu!!

That's my report for today.

https://youtu.be/L99bPMIMsBc?si=2Fcg4M8n-PI7hVVx

title_en: I Took the English Proficiency Test
excerpt_en: Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "taking the English proficiency test." Checking my English level. So, on the second day of my stay, there was orientation and a placement test for class assignments. It had 4 main parts
body_en:
Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "taking the English proficiency test."

Checking my English level

So, on the second day of my stay, there was orientation and a placement test for class assignments.

It had 4 main parts:

- Speaking test

- Listening test

- Reading test

- Writing test

It probably varies by school,

but I'll share about my school, "CELLA."

Speaking Test

4 students, 2 teachers.

We were given a level test textbook,

and one by one, we read passages out loud,

described illustrations,

introduced our favorite restaurants, and so on.

Based on our answers,

the examiners assessed our levels.

Just reading aloud was easy,

but I honestly didn't really understand what was written —

that told me exactly where my current level is.

Since they're looking at our current English ability,

I gave it everything I had,

and whatever class I get assigned to (even if it's low),

over these 3 months,

my goal is to level up properly

so I can understand what others are saying

and express my own thoughts too.

Listening Test

The listening test was the typical school test format —

multiple choice with 4 options.

I can't really say how difficult it was

since that depends on each person's ability,

but one thing that caught my attention was

that the test papers already had notes written on them,

so it seemed like they were reusing them without checking.

Also, the audio quality for the listening test wasn't great, so it was hard to hear.

Reading Test

The reading test had

long passages with questions for each,

testing whether you understood what was written.

This was also the typical school test format.

Writing Test

Lastly, the writing test.

In other words, writing.

There were two topics,

and you had to pick one and write about

"why is it that way?"

"what do you think about it?"

One of the topics this time was

"about how many people prefer to live in the same place,"

and I picked that one without hesitation.

The reason was simple — for the 2 years before coming to the Philippines,

I was living as an address hopper with no fixed home.

So, what were the results...??

More on that later!

Philippine ATMs are picky?

After the test, we had orientation,

and then the group that started this week

was taken shopping by car.

I went to an ATM to withdraw money for the on-site fees.

But the ATM wouldn't read my card,

and it kept spitting it back out.

When I asked around, apparently ATMs and cards have compatibility issues,

and sometimes they work and sometimes they don't.

It took me forever to get my withdrawal done.

Since I'm staying for 3 months,

I was told I'd need about 46,000 pesos.

That's about 130,000 yen in Japanese currency.

On top of that, with ATM fees and everything,

I ended up converting about 150,000 yen to pesos.

Studying abroad is expensive!!

But that just makes me all the more determined to study hard.

So, the test results...?

So,

about those test results:

I got absolutely destroyed. Lol

I was placed at the second-lowest level.

Everything except speaking

didn't go well at all.

That writing section where I was so confident —

turns out it was the lowest scoring section of all! Lol

The way I wrote about the address hopper lifestyle might partly be on me,

but it clearly didn't get through to the grader at all. Lol

So,

I was given materials matched to my level.

*Paid about 130,000 yen.

This includes 3 months of utility costs.

*The textbooks cost about 7,000 yen.

Classes start tomorrow,

but whether the textbooks match my level or not

can only be determined after actually using them,

so I was told not to write in them until about 3 days later.

So,

that's my write-up about day 2 at the language school.

I didn't study at all for the placement test,

so it measured my real, unfiltered English ability.

The bottom line: I found out that I have nothing but room for growth.

I'm going to study hard for 3 months

and get as close as possible to a level where I can talk business with people from overseas. Let's do this!!

https://youtu.be/i3Y5vOrMOOE?si=p7_zi81QSOrBulmI

excerpt_en: Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "classes beginning." Classes have finally started! So, starting from the 3rd day of my stay, classes at the language school have begun. Each class is 45 minutes with a 5-minute transition
body_en:
Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "classes beginning."

Classes have finally started!

So, starting from the 3rd day of my stay,

classes at the language school have begun.

Each class is 45 minutes with a 5-minute transition,

and there are 8 classes a day.

6 are one-on-one, and

2 are group lessons.

One-on-one classes are a hallmark of Philippine language schools,

and being able to have the teacher all to yourself during those sessions is definitely a big advantage.

This is my first time at a language school,

so I'll write exactly what I thought:

"Each teacher has their own unique style."

I expected everyone to just follow the textbook

mechanically,

but some teachers were really attentive to what I personally needed.

That said, it was the first day so there was also time for introductions,

so it's hard to say for sure..

The one-on-one teachers were mainly Filipino, but there was also one teacher from Malaysia.

The private rooms look like this,

and that's where you have your lessons.

I had 6 one-on-one classes just today, and

I really felt how important it is to put yourself in a situation where you're forced to speak English.

Honestly, the typical Japanese tendency of

"not speaking because you don't know the grammar"

is something I moved past through traveling overseas since my student days —

I understand that perfect grammar isn't necessary for communication.

In my case,

I have the attitude of

"who cares about grammar, as long as the message gets across!"

But that said,

a teacher pointed out during class that

"that makes it hard to have a proper back-and-forth conversation."

For sure —

when I went to Malaysia,

after shooting photos with models,

I'd often get invited to meals or cafes,

but since I was communicating with just words and short phrases rather than grammar,

I couldn't get into deep conversations.

Grammar is indeed necessary to some degree.

I don't think you need perfect grammar,

but I realized there's no downside to knowing the basics.

What about group lessons?

Then there are the 2 group lessons per day.

[Other countries mostly do group lessons.]

Especially after having one-on-one classes,

the group lessons feel a bit lacking

since the time isn't dedicated just to you.

You can learn from listening to others' conversations

and treating them as your own,

but being able to ask questions immediately in one-on-one lessons is definitely a big advantage.

I took both group lessons today —

one was with a female teacher,

and the other was with a male American teacher,

but the male teacher seemed pretty aloof.

You can freely "swap" teachers

So, with 8 classes a day,

meaning 8 different teachers on weekdays,

and the same teachers every day,

there are bound to be some who aren't a good fit.

For situations like that,

there's a teacher swap system.

Basically,

nothing changes unless you request it,

but you can submit a request.

So,

I've already put in a request to swap one teacher.

There was one male teacher in my one-on-one classes,

and the conversation would stall,

and I found myself putting on a fake smile quite a bit.

If we were just friends,

I wouldn't mind at all,

but I'm here to study,

so I felt like I didn't need to spend energy on that.

Hearing from other students,

it sounds like there are many great teachers,

so — and I know this sounds a bit harsh —

I plan to keep swapping

until I find teachers that are a good fit for me!

Making myself known

From here, I'm not sure how useful this will be,

but during self-introductions,

I ran an A/B test.

*Here's an explanation of A/B testing

*Feel free to skip this

A/B testing is an experimental method that compares two variations, A and B, of a product or service. It's typically used to improve things like website design, email campaigns, and app features.

The basic steps of A/B testing are:

  • Set a goal: Clarify what you want to improve. For example, the goal might be to increase a website's conversion rate (the percentage of visitors who take a desired action like making a purchase).
  • Create variations: Create the original (Variation A) and a modified new version (Variation B). For example, changing the color of a website button could be the modification.

-

  • Random assignment: Randomly assign visitors to Group A and Group B. This prevents other factors from influencing the results.
  • Data collection and analysis: Measure the performance of both groups and analyze which is more effective toward the goal.
  • Apply the results: If B is found to be better than A, adopt B. If they're the same, keep A or run another test.

The key to A/B testing is changing only one element at a time. This allows you to pinpoint exactly which change was effective. It also promotes data-driven decision making, allowing you to take action based on objective results rather than intuition or guesswork.

[*ChatGPT used]

In this case,

I compared a pattern where I disclosed one piece of information

versus a pattern where I disclosed two or more,

and observed the reactions.

To begin with, I'm not a college student,

so I also have a job —

and on top of that, I'm a photographer who also does digital art

and runs a Japanese traditional craft "masu" brand,

so there's a lot of information to share.

Based on my impressions from self-introductions,

when I bring up the "masu,"

people recognize "me" more,

and find me interesting as a "person."

Teachers are people too,

and they're teaching mostly similar students all the time,

so having a point of differentiation

is probably a good thing.

Plus, in my case, I always wear

my masu badge, which is part of my identity.

I felt that creating a clear point of differentiation

and making them think "this guy is here because he needs English for his global business"

would help things move faster.

*This is only after the first day of classes, so who knows what'll happen.

But at the very least, putting out the message that "a somewhat unusual student has arrived!"

seems like a strong move.

Plus, the female teachers eat lunch together,

so gossip travels super fast [speaking from experience],

and even during break time

I want to get to the point where

they're already talking about me on their own.

https://www.notion.so

excerpt_en: Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "the four skills." The 4 skills you learn at school. So, after the introductory first day, classes have fully started. I'm in the speaking-focused course, but there are 4 skills to learn.
body_en:
Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "the four skills."

The 4 skills you learn at school

So, after the introductory first day,

classes have fully started.

I'm in the speaking-focused course,

but there are 4 skills to learn.

Speaking

Reading

Listening

Writing

For me,

speaking and listening are super important skills for interacting with people from overseas.

And I currently feel that reading is also necessary.

With AI and translation tools advancing so much these days,

I had kind of assumed that reading ability wasn't needed anymore. But if you don't understand grammar and vocabulary, you can't translate things in your head while speaking or listening, so I feel that reading skills remain necessary no matter how fast technology advances.

As for writing,

this is just my personal opinion,

but if the goal is to get good grades in school, I get it. But there's almost no occasion to use writing skills in a business setting.

So,

for now, I don't think I really need it.

It's still early,

so I can't say for sure,

but I'll attend classes properly for the rest of this week

and also check whether I can make changes.

The food situation at a Cebu language school

Right now, I'm attending a language school

and staying in a 4-person room.

There's no kitchen, of course,

but there is a cafeteria.

It's buffet-style

where you can take whatever you want, as much as you want, which is a lifesaver for someone like me who doesn't cook.

On weekdays when there are classes, there are 3 buffet meals,

and on days off, they serve brunch and dinner — 2 meals.

Maybe because there are a lot of Japanese students,

they always serve "rice."

Though it's more like Thai-style rice.

It's not bad,

but it's nothing particularly amazing either.

It's good enough to fill your stomach — that's the food at a language school.

Since it's a cafeteria, lots of people eat at the same time, so I share tables with people I've become friends with here, as well as people I don't know at all.

Right now, a big group from Tokai University is at the school, so the ratio of Japanese students is quite high.

Even so, I'm trying to communicate in English as much as possible.

I'm in my late 20s,

but I'm glad I still have the "freshness" to blend in with people in their early 20s.

And just like that, day 2 of classes is done.

Talking about classes for 3 months straight

wouldn't be interesting for either the subscribers

or for me as the writer,

so I need to think about how to keep getting new input

during these weekdays when not much changes.

Since I've committed to writing at least 1,000-2,000 words every day, I'll make sure my input stays fresh and interesting too!

excerpt_en: Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "leveraging experience." Applying lessons from Kuala Lumpur. Day 5 in Cebu. On weekdays there are classes, so I wake up at 6:30 AM and am at school from 8 AM to 5 PM
body_en:
Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "leveraging experience."

Applying lessons from Kuala Lumpur

Day 5 in Cebu.

On weekdays there are classes, so I wake up at 6:30 AM

and am at school from 8 AM to 5 PM.

During that time, it's nothing but English study,

so for anything besides English, I use spare moments, finish lunch early to squeeze in time before afternoon classes,

or work on my own business

after 5 PM.

Now, since there are classes on weekdays,

I basically don't go out.

*Going out after 5 PM is OK.

But on my days off,

I need to get out of the school

and do promotions to spread the masu brand "FOMUS."

I'm also a photographer,

and last September I spent 10 days in Kuala Lumpur.

You can read the Kuala Lumpur activity report here [paid]

https://fomus.official.ec/items/79388914

There, I did daily "Masu Photo" shoots — photos with Japanese residents and Malaysians holding masu — but

in a brand new place,

even if you approach random strangers and take photos,

you can get Masu Photos, but the viral effect is practically zero.

The same applies here in the Philippines —

going up to random people on the street and saying

"want to hold a masu and take a photo?"

doesn't really have much impact.

So I'm going to use the same strategy I used in Malaysia here too.

That strategy is "Instagram advertising."

Not many people know this, but

Instagram ads let you select the age, interests, gender, and region of your target audience, and then run ads specifically to Instagram users within those parameters.

In Malaysia last time, I ran ads for 3 days,

got inquiries from 70 people,

selected 10 who were right for Masu Photo, and did the shoots.

The result of all that was

the "Masu Photo Collection in Malaysia."

The free version is here:

https://masuphoto.theshop.jp/p/00004

That experience was huge for me,

and this time I'm doing the same thing in Cebu.

Here's the actual promotional content I ran as an ad:

As it happens, the school is off from Friday this week,

so I suddenly have a 3-day weekend.

That means I also have 3 days for shoots.

As an experiment,

I only opened applications for this weekend.

There are several reasons:

if the shoot is more than 2 weeks away, people are likely to reschedule even if you stay in touch, and running ads for a longer period

means higher costs.

So,

I've actually been running ads on the FOMUS official account since the 6th [2 days ago].

Since I didn't know if the ads would actually be effective,

I set a low daily reach

and tested it at 1,000 yen per day.

As a result, I got Masu Photo offers from 21 people.

That's pretty solid.

However, I also got inquiries from private accounts and accounts with 0 followers, so I'm mostly not responding to those.

So, through steady back-and-forth communication

and schedule coordination,

I currently have 3 shoots confirmed.

A few more should be added from here,

and of course, there's always the possibility of last-minute cancellations.

But even that makes for good content, honestly,

so either way, it works out.

When you take action, something always happens [or you make it happen],

so taking action is definitely important!

So,

today I talked about one strategy I'm using to recruit Masu Photo subjects while in Cebu.

Outside of people who own physical shops,

I doubt many people are doing things this way. Lol

I hope this is useful for someone!

title_en: Masu Photo: The Ultimate Communication Tool
excerpt_en: Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Masu Photo: the ultimate communication tool." It begins! So, my first day off from school since arriving in Cebu has come. As I wrote in the previous article, I'd been using Instagram to find models based in Cebu
body_en:
Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Masu Photo: the ultimate communication tool."

It begins!

So, my first day off from school since arriving in Cebu has come.

As I wrote in the previous article,

I'd been using Instagram

to find models based in Cebu.

It was a nerve-wracking situation — would they actually show up?

The result was...

The model actually showed up!!

And she was absolutely gorgeous!

She was about 10 minutes late from the scheduled time,

but we successfully met up and were able to do the Masu Photo shoot.

As for the shoot location,

I had no idea about photo spots in Cebu,

so I asked her for her recommendations

and we headed there by Grab [a taxi service in the Philippines].

The cafe we went to was

a place called Merci Cafe,

with a cafe on the 1st floor and a photo spot on the 2nd floor.

First, we had a light chat on the 1st floor

to break the ice,

then went up to the 2nd floor for the shoot (Masu Photo).

At FOMUS, in addition to the regular logo-stamped masu, we also have

an accessory brand called "FOMUS Parure" that started in 2023,

and I brought a whole bunch this time.

All of the Parure accessories are

handcrafted by Yuri, who is also a FOMUS model.

This time too, the model wore the accessories, and we got some perfect Masu Photos.

After the shoot, we relaxed at the 1st floor cafe.

The conversation was, of course, in English.

After 5 days of hearing nothing but English every day,

my ears had gotten a bit used to it, and I was able to

understand what she was saying to a reasonable degree

and communicate.

Attending the school is definitely paying off! Lol

Talking with her,

she turned out to be 19 years old and studying at a medical school.

When she asked my age and I said "28,"

she was absolutely shocked.

"I thought you were around my age."

"You look so young!"

I'm not sure if this is a good or bad thing for business,

but when it comes to photo shoots,

it's working in my favor.

Maybe being a photographer really is my calling.

And that's not all!

I wasn't going to stop there.

From here, I headed off to shoot one more person.

The location was about a 20-minute walk from the first shoot — a place called "Cebu Premium Park."

I chose it just based on the name.

But here, I made a mistake.

It turns out,

"Cebu Premium Park" was a cemetery.

I had called a model to a cemetery.

No photographer starts a shoot

at a cemetery as the meeting point.

And I didn't want to shoot there either.

So we met up and immediately relocated.

There was a shopping mall nearby,

so we did the shoot there.

As always, I chat while shooting,

and this person was also 19.

I don't ask for age or any background info beforehand

when scheduling shoots,

so I really only find out once I get there.

That's part of the fun.

"Masu Photo" — the ultimate communication tool

So anyway,

the shoots wrapped up successfully,

and after each shoot,

since both models had time, we went to cafes.

We spoke in English, naturally.

I always ask them why they agreed to do the shoot,

and the first model said:

"A Japanese person carrying Japanese traditional crafts and doing photo shoots popped up on my Instagram stories [the ad], and my friend recommended it, so I reached out. The concept sounded really interesting."

The second model said:

"I saw it on my Instagram stories — someone from Japan, which I love, coming to shoot photos with Japanese content, so I applied."

In both cases, the "masu" was the trigger.

In other words, they likely wouldn't have responded

if it were just a photo shoot.

I used the same strategy in Malaysia,

and sure enough, here in Cebu too,

the "masu" as a communication tool is a big deal.

And for me, the biggest point is

being able to interact with them after the shoot.

That's when I can hear their stories in detail,

and tell them about myself and FOMUS.

Normally, if you come to the Philippines,

you're not going to meet random local models on day 6.

And they get to learn about FOMUS,

and they're saying they'll tell their friends about it!

FOMUS has been going for 3 full years now.

It's been worth continuing,

and watching the brand we've built gradually spread around the world is deeply moving.

I have another shoot tomorrow.

Let's do this!

Can't skip bar-hopping either

After the shoots, I headed to a bar rumored to have a Japanese owner.

I went for a drink alone.

But I couldn't find the Japanese owner.

https://www.notion.so

After that, some Japanese classmates from the same school in Cebu joined me,

and 4 of us ended up drinking together.

Of course, since we're here to learn English,

everyone was speaking English.

Having buddies like this is really great.

To keep spreading FOMUS,

I'm going to show up everywhere

and steadily expand its reach!!

https://www.notion.so

excerpt_en: Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "learning about the culture." You learn about the culture by talking with locals. Today is Saturday. No school. So, today I went out for Masu Photo shoots again.
body_en:
Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "learning about the culture."

You learn about the culture by talking with locals

Today is Saturday. No school.

So, today I went out for Masu Photo shoots again.

I shot with 3 people today.

I won't go into detail about each one,

but what everyone said unanimously was:

"I saw it on an Instagram story, and I didn't really know what 'masu' was, but

I'm interested in Japanese traditional crafts, so I applied."

That means 5 out of 5 people were

drawn by the "masu."

It'll take more time to gather enough data,

but what I've confirmed is:

FOMUS's Instagram ads

resonate with people in Southeast Asia.

I'm now confident about that.

This time too, all shoot locations were chosen by the models.

At Ayala Center, one of the biggest shopping malls in Cebu, I shot with the first person.

She turned out to be 18, and her father came along too.

Her father watched the shoot from a short distance away.

She also showed me a hidden spot nearby — not in the shopping mall — a place with a pool.

We got some great shots in a nice atmosphere.

After the shoot,

the three of us — me, the model, and her father — talked.

Her father seemed to be a really sharp businessman,

and he was very interested in the masu.

Also,

when I heard about his daughter's birthday,

I learned that in the Philippines, for a daughter's 18th birthday,

they throw a party on the level of a wedding reception.

*The birthday celebration Filipinos spend the most money on is a girl's 18th birthday, called a "Debut."

He showed me a video,

and the scale was just incredible!

They rent out a huge banquet hall for a party,

complete with well-produced interview videos and everything.

It's several levels above a Japanese coming-of-age ceremony.

The father looked so happy talking about his daughter,

and I thought — this is universal, no matter where in the world you are.

Since they both came all the way out,

I gave them a one-go masu and a mini masu as gifts.

Just talk as much as possible

The language school I'm attending

currently has quite a few Japanese students.

They're all university sophomores and juniors in their early 20s.

Basically, everyone tries to speak in English, but when there are tricky nuances, we sometimes switch to Japanese.

On weekends, I usually go out alone,

but since I often take Grab taxis from school to the shoot locations, some students will ride along to head out at the same time as my shoots.

We split the Grab fare,

so it works out for everyone.

Once we arrive, we go our separate ways.

Everyone goes to hang out, and I go to my shoots.

It's only been 2 days,

but I've managed to create tons of opportunities

to speak and listen to English.

I've never heard of a language student using Instagram to find people to talk to,

but in my case,

spreading FOMUS is the top priority,

and the English improvement that comes along with it

is a great bonus — two birds with one stone.

I've literally been talking all day, every day,

and my brain can't keep up with processing everything.

As I'm writing this article, I think I might have a slight fever.

*I haven't measured, so I'm not sure.

That's how much information I'm taking in.

The Philippine climate probably plays a role too.

I've learned that weekends dedicated to speaking and listening

take a heavy toll on your stamina.

If I'm getting worn out at this level,

I've got a long way to go,

so I need to make sure I recover properly.

Tomorrow is the last day of the 3-day weekend.

I have one shoot scheduled for tomorrow.

I'll keep spreading FOMUS!

excerpt_en: Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "why I do Masu Photo." Why am I doing Masu Photo in the Philippines? So, I had another shoot today, and I successfully shot with 6 people over the 3 days.
body_en:
Hey, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "why I do Masu Photo."

Why am I doing Masu Photo in the Philippines?

So, I had another shoot today,

and I successfully shot with 6 people over the 3 days.

Some people showed up late,

but nobody completely bailed,

so that was great!

So why am I doing Masu Photo shoots in the first place?

Simply put,

it's to spread the masu brand "FOMUS."

There are people in Japan

who use FOMUS in their own activities.

That makes me really happy.

However, including myself,

activities within Japan just don't reach overseas at all.

What we do is too niche, too specialized,

and too cutting-edge.

FOMUS calls itself a masu brand,

but it also functions as a "community."

FOMUS events and masu-related content

serve as "touchpoints (opportunities)" for people to gather,

and the people who come together

aren't just talking about FOMUS

the whole time.

They talk about their own products and services, listen to others,

share hobbies,

and all sorts of free-flowing communication happens.

What we at "FOMUS" provide is

a "place" and a "community,"

and even more than that, "shared memories."

As of 2024,

everyone can broadcast themselves,

and with AI advancing, anyone can become a creator.

In other words, there's way too much content out there.

That's exactly why at FOMUS,

we center everything around "masu" — a Japanese traditional craft —

and incorporate it through photos, events, cuisine, art, characters, card games, and various other forms,

strengthening the FOMUS brand community.

Going forward,

community will be incredibly important,

and I believe that "history," "story,"

and the "memories" of everyone involved with FOMUS will become something even greater.

"Masu Photo" (the photo shoots) is one part of that.

Choosing content that works worldwide

When I started FOMUS,

I decided it would be a brand that works on the global stage.

So, what I printed on the Japanese traditional craft "masu" was the "FOMUS" logo.

Outside of commemorative items, the companies that put branding on masu are mostly sake breweries, so they're basically all in "kanji."

That does appeal to foreigners in its own way,

but at the end of the day, it's content with a strong "free gift that comes with sake" connotation,

so no real communication is born from it.

With FOMUS,

if you're holding one of these masu,

there's a chance you can connect with others

who are involved with FOMUS in other ways.

In Japan, people who've engaged with FOMUS through various channels actually come together at FOMUS-organized events, and real communication is born.

There's a common thread there:

the content FOMUS creates is

either "non-verbal" or "has a low translation barrier."

The card game (SILVA)

has a low translation barrier,

and once you learn the rules, you can play across borders.

https://youtu.be/Tn4nYx-s7Kw?si=ylgI5nekqCgqkytQ

And photography (Masu Photo) is a non-verbal tool.

Of course, you use language to communicate during the actual shoot, but once the photo is taken, it's just something you look at — no language needed.

And tying this back

to everything I said earlier,

the reason I'm doing Masu Photo in Cebu is

to get as many people in Cebu as possible

to know about the brand

called FOMUS.

At our current level, even if we post about FOMUS activities, it's hard to reach people overseas.

But with Masu Photo, everyone shares the photos on their Instagram stories while holding the masu.

That means FOMUS reaches

their followers.

In fact, I've already gotten messages

from friends of people who did the Masu Photo shoots.

In other words, through Masu Photo,

people who didn't know FOMUS now know it exists.

Honestly, it might not be the most efficient method.

But the fact that the head of FOMUS personally comes to the location and engages with each person one-on-one

is something people genuinely appreciate.

It's a low-key, physically demanding activity,

but for these 3 months, I'll keep steadily working to spread FOMUS.

Starting tomorrow, it's back to weekdays, which means "language school."

I'll study English hard too!!

https://youtu.be/r-GgJIUH5y0?si=v0C0RxmN_bo_yTOL
